preFix.title: Insulating the eaves zone in a pitched roof loft
preFix.subtitle: Filling the wedge-shaped gap where the insulation meets the rafters without blocking ventilation
preFix.excerpt: The eaves zone, the triangular area where a pitched roof meets the top of the external wall, is the most frequently botched detail in a loft insulation top-up. Insulation pushed right to the eaves blocks the ventilation cross-flow that prevents interstitial condensation on the felt. The correct detail uses eaves baffles to maintain a 50 mm clear channel while extending the insulation to the top of the wall plate.
preFix.sourceNotes: NHBC Technical Guidance Chapter 7.1, loft insulation at eaves. Building Regulations Part C (Site Preparation and Resistance to Contaminants and Moisture) 2004. BBA Approved Document for eaves ventilation products.

content.text: A standard loft insulation top-up brings insulation up to 270 to 300 mm above the ceiling joists. The problem at the eaves is geometry: the roofline descends and the available depth reduces to zero at the
. If the insulation is simply pushed to fill this wedge, it blocks the eaves vent and traps moisture beneath the felt, leading to

Building Regulations require a minimum 25 mm clear air path at the eaves for a pitched roof below 15° pitch; 50 mm for steeper pitches. The 50 mm clear channel is the default to aim for, it provides a safety margin and is what the eaves baffle products are designed to maintain.
Measure the rafter spacing at the eaves zone, typically 400 mm or 600 mm centres. Buy
to match. They are available as rigid polypropylene or recycled cardboard channels, typically 1,200 mm long and sold to suit standard rafter centres.

Working from the access hatch, crawl carefully onto the joists (never stand between joists, only on them) and push the baffle between the rafters down toward the eaves. The baffle should rest on the top of the insulation already present and extend down to within 50 mm of the eaves vent. Staple the baffle to the rafters on each side with a tacker or a staple gun.

With the baffle in place, pack the insulation into the wedge zone between the ceiling joist and the baffle. Use loose-fill mineral wool or cut batts to fit. The insulation should fill the zone from the wall plate to the top of the baffle, with no gaps.

Check all eaves baffles are correctly seated and the vent pathway is clear before laying the top-up insulation across the joist zone. The top-up layer runs over the ceiling joists and covers the top of the baffles, but must not collapse into the baffle channel.
What it looks like done well versus done badly

Done well: a clear 50 mm channel runs from each eaves vent up past the insulation. The insulation fills the eaves zone below the baffle. The top-up insulation lies flat across the joist zone. In a thermal image from outside in cold weather, the underside of the eaves shows an even temperature strip, confirming insulation is continuous to the wall plate.
Done badly: insulation pushed into the eaves blocks the vent. Cold air bridges appear at each rafter bay. The felt is damp underneath when inspected in summer after a wet winter.
